topic: Topological photovoltaics and mathematical condensed matter

items.title: The bulk photovoltaic effect in one-dimensional tellurium nanowires for self-powered broadband polarization-sensitive photodetection
items.tldr: A single tellurium nanowire produces a zero-bias broadband photovoltaic response and polarization-sensitive detection from 405 to 1550 nm.
items.summary: The authors report an intrinsic bulk photovoltaic effect in individual one-dimensional tellurium nanowires and use it for self-powered photodetection. The device operates without external bias across wavelengths from 405 to 1550 nm, reaching a reported responsivity of 5.9 mA/W and detectivity of 1.85 x 10^11 Jones at 1550 nm. Its anisotropic response gives a polarization ratio as high as 2.33 and supports both polarization-encoded optical communication and spatially resolved photocurrent imaging. The abstract establishes the device-scale BPVE and performance metrics, but it does not identify whether shift, injection, ballistic, or contact-related contributions dominate the measured current.
items.research_relevance: This is the week's most direct experimental connection to the research program. Tellurium nanowires provide a concrete quasi-one-dimensional platform in which symmetry, anisotropy, and microscopic photocurrent mechanisms can be compared. A useful follow-up would separate intrinsic shift or injection terms from scattering-dependent ballistic and device-contact contributions rather than treating BPVE as a single mechanism.
items.potential_use: Use the reported wavelength, polarization, and zero-bias data as an experimental benchmark for a mechanism-resolved calculation of tellurium photocurrent.
photocurrent_mechanisms: intrinsic bulk photovoltaic effect; microscopic channel unresolved in the abstract
items.noncentrosymmetry_evidence: Not stated explicitly in the supplied abstract; no noncentrosymmetric-topological highlight is assigned.
items.evidence_basis: Direct device-level BPVE measurements in single Te nanowires, including zero-bias response, wavelength range, and polarization metrics.
items.key_strength: It is the only supplied candidate with a direct bulk-photovoltaic experiment and quantitative device benchmark.
items.key_limitation: The abstract does not resolve shift, injection, ballistic, impurity-assisted, excitation, recombination, or contact contributions.
items.critical_assessment: This remains the strongest match because it directly measures zero-bias photocurrent in individual Te nanowires with quantitative spectral and polarization benchmarks. However, zero bias and material noncentrosymmetry do not by themselves establish an intrinsic bulk mechanism or exclude contact fields, asymmetric collection, heating, or scattering-dependent transport. The supplied evidence contains no mechanism-resolving dependence on device length, contact configuration, relaxation time, or polarization tensor components. It is therefore compelling phenomenology and a useful benchmark, but insufficient evidence for assigning shift, injection, ballistic, or other microscopic channels.

items.title: Monopole Spin Density Wave States in Magnetic Weyl Semimetals
items.tldr: Monopole spin-density-wave order in magnetic Weyl semimetals carries a pairing Berry phase, protected gap nodes, and ideal quantum geometry in the weak-coupling limit.
items.summary: This theory introduces monopole spin-density-wave states formed by particle-hole pairing between nested Fermi surfaces surrounding Weyl nodes of equal chirality. The resulting order parameter inherits a nontrivial pairing Berry phase and is represented by monopole harmonics, producing topologically protected nodal structures in the gap. A lattice model distinguishes helical and cycloidal orders through band dispersions, Fermi arcs, and surface spin-polarization patterns that could be probed by spin-resolved photoemission. The paper also finds ideal quantum geometry for the monopole-pairing quantum geometric tensor at weak coupling, where quantum-distance fluctuations are fixed by Berry curvature, and proposes rare-earth ReAlX compounds as candidate platforms.
items.research_relevance: The ideal relation between quantum metric information and Berry curvature is relevant to attempts to organize nonlinear response by quantum geometry. Although this paper does not calculate photovoltaic current, its monopole-harmonic construction and pairing Berry phase offer a mathematically controlled setting for asking whether ordered Weyl phases acquire distinctive nonlinear optical or photocurrent signatures.
items.potential_use: Speculatively, adapt the lattice model to compute second-order optical tensors across the helical and cycloidal ordered phases.
items.noncentrosymmetry_evidence: No bulk inversion-symmetry claim is made in the supplied abstract.
items.evidence_basis: Monopole-harmonic SDW order in a lattice Weyl model, pairing Berry phase, protected gap nodes, and ideal quantum geometry in weak coupling.
items.key_strength: It gives a controlled quantum-geometric construction linking Berry curvature and quantum distance in an ordered Weyl setting.
items.key_limitation: The paper does not calculate photovoltaic or second-order optical response, and the ReAlX connection is presented as a candidate material direction.
items.critical_assessment: The controlled monopole-harmonic order and weak-coupling relation between pairing Berry curvature and quantum-distance fluctuations are mathematically substantive. Their photovoltaic relevance is nevertheless unproven: the reported quantum geometry belongs to the particle-hole pairing structure and cannot automatically be substituted for the electronic band geometry entering nonlinear optical matrix elements. No light-matter coupling, second-order conductivity, inversion-symmetry analysis of the ordered phase, or relaxation mechanism is supplied. ReAlX is only a proposed platform, so neither material realization nor an enhanced photocurrent follows from the abstract.

items.title: Rotation topological states: theory and material realization
items.tldr: Decomposing occupied bands by rotation eigenvalue reveals hidden Z2^n topology and surface double-Weyl points that conventional occupied-space invariants miss.
items.summary: The work proposes a rotation-subspace classification for band topology. Instead of evaluating a single invariant over all occupied bands, the occupied Hilbert space is decomposed into sectors labeled by rotation eigenvalues, each carrying its own Z2 index. Time-reversal symmetry pairs conjugate sectors and can make their nontrivial indices invisible to the conventional global Z2 invariant. First-principles calculations identify bulk CsCl as globally trivial by standard diagnostics but nontrivial in several rotation subspaces along high-symmetry lines. The predicted consequence is a set of double-Weyl surface points on the (111) and (001) surfaces. The result is a symmetry-refined diagnostic rather than a photovoltaic-response calculation.
items.research_relevance: This is directly relevant to delicate and multigap invariants because it demonstrates how topology can disappear after subspaces are recombined. The Z2^n structure provides a new mathematical object to compare with Hopf, returning-Thouless-pump, and band-addition-sensitive classifications, and it suggests testing whether nonlinear optical response can resolve topology hidden from the total occupied bundle.
items.potential_use: Use the rotation-sector projector construction as a candidate basis for symmetry-resolved shift-current or injection-current calculations.
items.noncentrosymmetry_evidence: The abstract does not establish broken bulk inversion symmetry, so the material is not tagged as noncentrosymmetric.
items.evidence_basis: A proposed rotation-subspace Z2^n classification, with bulk CsCl calculations and predicted surface double-Weyl points.
items.key_strength: It supplies a concrete band-decomposition invariant relevant to hidden or delicate topology.
items.key_limitation: No nonlinear optical or photovoltaic response is calculated, and the supplied abstract does not establish bulk noncentrosymmetry.
items.critical_assessment: The rotation-sector classification is unusually specific and directly relevant to topology that disappears when occupied subspaces are recombined. Yet hidden sector indices and predicted surface double-Weyl points do not imply a bulk photovoltaic signature. Establishing that transfer would require a symmetry-allowed bulk second-order tensor, optical selection rules that resolve rotation sectors, and a calculation showing that sector contributions avoid cancellation. None is reported. The CsCl calculation supports the proposed topological diagnosis, but the supplied evidence neither establishes bulk inversion breaking nor connects the surface states to measurable bulk photocurrent.

items.title: ZPAN: An Organic Nonlinear Optical Crystal for High Intensity THz Generation
items.tldr: Noncentrosymmetric ZPAN crystals combine scalable growth, a polar axis, and strong optical rectification to generate broadband terahertz fields near 1 MV/cm.
items.summary: The authors optimize synthesis and crystal growth for the organic nonlinear-optical material ZPAN and explicitly obtain noncentrosymmetric packing by slow evaporation from acetone-based solvents. The resulting crystals grow as long prisms whose [001] polar axis is also the preferred pump-polarization direction for terahertz generation by optical rectification. Across near-infrared pump wavelengths, thicknesses, and powers, the crystals produce a smooth 0.5-3.4 THz spectrum and peak-to-peak fields approaching 1 MV/cm. Calculated second-order coefficients indicate that two crystal faces can support generation, while the large dominant growth face offers a route to scaling the illuminated aperture for high-power systems.
items.research_relevance: ZPAN is a useful control case for strong second-order response created by explicit noncentrosymmetric packing without a stated topological mechanism. Comparing its symmetry-allowed optical-rectification tensor with band-geometric shift-current formulas can help separate generic polar-crystal enhancement from response that is genuinely tied to Berry geometry or band topology in realistic device-scale crystals.
items.potential_use: Use ZPAN as a nontopological noncentrosymmetric benchmark when evaluating whether a proposed topological material offers a distinct nonlinear-response advantage.
items.noncentrosymmetry_evidence: The abstract explicitly reports noncentrosymmetric crystal packing and a [001] polar axis.
items.evidence_basis: Explicit noncentrosymmetric ZPAN crystal packing, polar axis, optical-rectification measurements, and broadband THz output near 1 MV/cm.
items.key_strength: It provides a concrete noncentrosymmetric nonlinear-optics control case with scale-up-relevant measurements.
items.key_limitation: It is not a topological material study and optical rectification is not equivalent to a bulk photovoltaic current.
items.critical_assessment: ZPAN is a credible control for strong second-order response because noncentrosymmetric packing, pump orientation, bandwidth, and emitted THz fields are directly characterized. Its value for photovoltaic research must remain narrowly framed. Optical rectification measures a driven nonlinear polarization and emitted field, not transported dc charge; large THz output therefore cannot be interpreted as large shift current or favorable carrier extraction. Molecular-crystal resonances, phase matching, absorption, thickness, and aperture scaling may govern performance independently of band topology. It benchmarks ordinary polar nonlinear optics, not a competing photovoltaic mechanism.

items.title: Magnetic quadrupole current generation and accumulation in noncentrosymmetric systems
items.tldr: Symmetry permits magnetic-quadrupole currents broadly in noncentrosymmetric point groups and predicts measurable edge accumulation in noncentrosymmetric antiferromagnets.
items.summary: This paper develops a symmetry theory for generating magnetic-quadrupole currents in noncentrosymmetric antiferromagnets. By decomposing the magnetic-quadrupole conductivity tensor into multipole representations, it identifies the crystallographic point groups in which such currents are allowed. A representative model follows symmetry lowering from centrosymmetric mmm to noncentrosymmetric mm2 and demonstrates linear-response current generation. The theory further predicts magnetic-quadrupole accumulation near sample boundaries, analogous to spin accumulation in the spin Hall effect, providing a possible experimental signature and a prerequisite for quadrupole injection. The work concerns magnetic-multipole transport rather than optical bulk photovoltaic current, but its symmetry machinery is broadly transferable.
items.research_relevance: The tensor and point-group analysis provides a useful template for classifying photovoltaic response beyond scalar symmetry labels. It could help organize impurity-assisted or ballistic photocurrent channels when magnetic multipoles and broken inversion coexist, while the edge-accumulation result illustrates how a bulk response can be connected to a distinct boundary observable.
items.potential_use: Adapt the multipole decomposition to classify second-order photocurrent tensors in noncentrosymmetric antiferromagnets.
items.noncentrosymmetry_evidence: Noncentrosymmetric crystallographic point groups and the mm2 example are explicit in the abstract.
items.evidence_basis: Point-group and multipole-conductivity analysis for magnetic-quadrupole currents, including an mmm-to-mm2 example and edge accumulation.
items.key_strength: The symmetry decomposition is transferable to classifying tensor response in noncentrosymmetric magnetic systems.
items.key_limitation: The work treats linear magnetic-multipole transport, not optical BPVE, and no photocurrent channel is identified.
items.critical_assessment: The multipole and point-group decomposition is a legitimate methodological reference, especially for relating a bulk magnetic-quadrupole current to boundary accumulation. Transfer to photovoltaic classification is not automatic, however. The studied conductivity is linear and multipole-specific, whereas optical bulk-photovoltaic response is frequency-dependent, second order in the electromagnetic field, and constrained by additional permutation, time-reversal, and dissipation properties. No optical vertex, carrier generation process, or impurity-assisted or ballistic channel is derived. Its ranking below the measured nonlinear-optics control is therefore justified; the relevance is formal rather than mechanistic.

items.title: Emergent induction in magnetic Weyl semimetals
items.tldr: Magnetization dynamics in magnetic Weyl semimetals produces emergent induction with relaxation-time-dependent intraband and Weyl-node-separation-dependent interband contributions.
items.summary: The authors formulate emergent electromagnetic induction generated by magnetic dynamics in Weyl semimetals. Starting from two-band models, they derive a magnetoelectric response with distinct intraband and interband components. The intraband term scales with the relaxation time, whereas the interband term is tied to the separation of Weyl nodes. In a model of a polar Weyl ferromagnet, toroidal-moment dynamics controls the induced response, and its chemical-potential dependence is enhanced over the Weyl-dispersion energy range. The calculation is not a photovoltaic theory, but it clearly exposes how kinetic and band-structure contributions coexist in a topological material response and how they can be distinguished parametrically.
items.research_relevance: The separation between a relaxation-time-dependent intraband term and an interband term controlled by Weyl-node geometry parallels the distinction between ballistic or scattering-sensitive photocurrent and intrinsic interband shift or injection response. The polar Weyl model may therefore provide a useful conceptual testbed for mechanism-resolved topological photovoltaic calculations and their chemical-potential dependence.
items.potential_use: Compare the paper's intraband/interband decomposition with impurity-assisted and intrinsic terms in a Weyl-semimetal photocurrent calculation.
items.noncentrosymmetry_evidence: A polar Weyl-ferromagnet model is explicit, but no specific bulk material is established as both topological and noncentrosymmetric in the abstract.
items.evidence_basis: Two-band theory separating relaxation-time-dependent intraband and Weyl-node-separation-dependent interband magnetoelectric induction.
items.key_strength: It cleanly separates kinetic and interband geometric contributions in a Weyl response.
items.key_limitation: Emergent induction from magnetization dynamics is not a photovoltaic theory and the polar Weyl model is not a demonstrated material realization.
items.critical_assessment: The explicit separation of relaxation-time-dependent intraband induction from an interband term tied to Weyl-node separation is useful for thinking about mechanism-resolved response. It remains only an analogy to photovoltaics because magnetic dynamics and optical excitation involve different perturbations, response functions, selection rules, and dc limits. In particular, an interband contribution controlled by node separation is not evidence for shift or injection current, while relaxation-time scaling alone does not identify a ballistic photocurrent. The polar Weyl ferromagnet is a model rather than an established material platform, supporting its lowest retained rank.

ranking_basis: Ranks prioritize direct target-phenomenon evidence, then mechanism/invariant specificity, then transfer value; limitations break ties.
